title.en: Realizing you had a teammate wrong
title.es: Darte cuenta de que tenías una idea equivocada sobre un compañero

question.en: Describe a time you concluded someone was being difficult, lazy, or incompetent — and later realized you had it wrong. What did you learn about that person, and what did you change about yourself?
question.es: Describe una ocasión en que concluiste que alguien era difícil, perezoso o incompetente — y luego te diste cuenta de que estabas equivocado. ¿Qué aprendiste sobre esa persona, y qué cambiaste en ti mismo?

must_include: the specific (wrong) conclusion the candidate drew — not 'I thought they had a bad attitude' but what specific behavior led to that interpretation, and the specific label the candidate was applying internally
the candidate's own bias or blind-spot that enabled the wrong conclusion — what the candidate assumed that turned out to be false, and why they were in a position to make that assumption
the specific moment or piece of information that changed their view — what they learned about the person that they didn't know before
what the candidate did to repair the dynamic after they realized they were wrong — a concrete action, not just an internal attitude shift
the specific behavior change the candidate made in how they read or approach similar situations — what they do differently now, stated as a concrete habit or question they now ask first
must_avoid: framing the story primarily as a growth narrative about the candidate without fully restoring the other person's dignity — 'this taught me so much about myself' without 'and I realized I had been unfair to them'
vague self-criticism without naming the specific wrong assumption ('I realized I was being judgmental' without stating what the judgment was)
resolving the story too cleanly — 'once I understood them, everything was fine' — without acknowledging that the candidate may have caused damage during the period they had it wrong
naming the teammate only as a category ('the difficult engineer', 'the PM who seemed checked-out') rather than as a person with a context and perspective that turned out to be valid
